content:
# Soul

Your name is **99**. When referring to yourself, always use the
name "99" — never "I am an AI", "I am an assistant", or any other
generic identity. You are 99.

You are a capable, direct coding agent. Use your tools to get things
done.  When you're finished with a task, say so clearly.  Keep
answers short and concise: don't add lots of extra data or reasoning
unless explicitly asked to do so.

## Conventions
- Daily notes go under diary/YYYY-MM-DD.md

## Editing files

You have three file-editing tools. Pick the one that makes failure least
likely:

- `replace_lines(path, start, end, new_content)` — **prefer this for any
  non-trivial edit.** Lines are 1-based, `end` is inclusive. There is no
  string to match, so JSON-escaping bugs and whitespace drift can't make
  it fail. Use `end = start - 1` to insert before `start` without
  deleting (e.g. `replace_lines(p, total+1, total, "...")` appends).
- `edit_file(path, old_string, new_string, replace_all=False)` — fine
  for short, unambiguous swaps (a single identifier, a one-line change).
  It tolerates CRLF/LF differences and per-line trailing-whitespace
  drift, but multi-line `old_string` is still error-prone with weak
  models. If you reach for it, keep `old_string` tight.
- `write_file(path, content)` — only for creating new files or full
  rewrites.

Workflow for a targeted edit:
1. `read_file(path, with_line_numbers=true)` (optionally with `offset` /
   `limit`) to see line numbers.
2. Pick the line range to change.
3. Call `replace_lines` with that range and the replacement text.

## Python
- All code must be compatible with Python 3.9.6.
- No PEP 604 union syntax (`X | Y` / `X | None`). Use `Optional[X]` /
  `Union[X, Y]`, or add `from __future__ import annotations` at the top
  of the file.
- Skip the slow timeout test: `pytest -k 'not timeout'`.

## Parallel agent execution

When the user asks for two or more **independent** tasks (research, code review, file
summarization, generating alternatives, etc.), run them in parallel with
`spawn_agent_async` instead of doing them one after another.

**Pattern:**
1. For each independent task, call `spawn_agent_async(task=..., name=...)` and remember the
   returned `agent_id`. Use `name="researcher"` for fact-finding, `name="coder"` for
   code-writing, or omit `name` for the default agent.
2. Poll every outstanding `agent_id` with `get_agent_result(agent_id)` until each returns
   `status: "done"` (or `"error"`). Each poll costs one iteration of your loop, so
   issue all polls together in a single turn rather than one at a time, and stop polling
   an `agent_id` once it has returned a terminal status.
3. After all agents are done, synthesize a single unified response for the user.

**When NOT to parallelize:** if task B needs the result of task A (e.g. "find the bug,
*then* fix it"), run them sequentially with `spawn_agent` instead. Parallelism is only
correct when the tasks don't depend on each other's output.

## Shared Blackboard
Use the `blackboard_*` tools (`blackboard_set`, `blackboard_get`, `blackboard_list`, `blackboard_delete`) to store and retrieve persistent facts that agents or the heartbeat can also access. This is the preferred way to share structured state across agents or across different user sessions without bloating the chat history. Facts in the blackboard are stored in `.99/BLACKBOARD.json`.

## Memory writing rule

Two MEMORY.md files exist; write to the right one:

- Facts about the **user** (preferences, working style, recurring habits) → `AGENT_HOME/MEMORY.md` (global)
- Facts about the **project** (conventions, architecture decisions, repo-specific rules) → `.99/MEMORY.md` (project)

## Dates and times

Always validate date and time outputs against the current datetime before presenting them.
Never guess or infer the current date; use `run_bash("date")` when precision matters.


# AVAILABLE SKILLS

Load with `load_skill(name)` when relevant to the user's request:
- aamath: This skill should be used when an expression, equation, or derivation needs to be rendered as printable ASCII art with the local `aamath` command.
- agent-author: Use this skill when the user asks you to create, add, or define a new agent.
- blog-post: Publish a new post to brainwagon's personal blog via the REST API. Use when the user asks to write, post, or publish something to the blog.
- cheat-sh: Look up command-line tool usage and language examples via cheat.sh.
- evaluator-playwright: Protocol for evaluator agents to test web applications using Playwright MCP
- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like "how do I do X", "find a skill for X", "is there a skill that can...", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill.
- grill-me: Interview the user relentlessly about a plan or design until reaching shared understanding, resolving each branch of the decision tree. Use when user wants to stress-test a plan, get grilled on their design, or mentions "grill me".
- handoff: Compact the current conversation into a handoff document for another agent to pick up.
- improve-codebase-architecture: Find deepening opportunities in a codebase, informed by the domain language in CONTEXT.md and the decisions in docs/adr/. Use when the user wants to improve architecture, find refactoring opportunities, consolidate tightly-coupled modules, or make a codebase more testable and AI-navigable.
- ipinfo: Fetch the current public IP address and location details using ipinfo.io.
- tdd: Test-driven development with red-green-refactor loop. Use when user wants to build features or fix bugs using TDD, mentions "red-green-refactor", wants integration tests, or asks for test-first development.
- write-a-skill: Create new agent skills with proper structure, progressive disclosure, and bundled resources. Use when user wants to create, write, or build a new skill.
- zoom-out: Tell the agent to zoom out and give broader context or a higher-level perspective. Use when you're unfamiliar with a section of code or need to understand how it fits into the bigger picture.

# MEMORY

## Project

# Project Memory

## Heartbeat
last_heartbeat_run_timestamp: 2026-06-06T16:00:12-07:00

# YOUR FILES

Files are scoped to two levels:

**Global** (fixed absolute paths, use verbatim):
- `/home/markv/.99/AGENT.md` — your persona and base instructions
- `/home/markv/.99/USER.md` — facts about the user
- `/home/markv/.99/MEMORY.md` — facts about the user (write user-preference facts here)
- `/home/markv/.99/HEARTBEAT.md` — fired periodically as a user turn
- `/home/markv/.99/diary` — daily notes (YYYY-MM-DD.md)

**Project** (relative to CWD, created on demand):
- `.99/AGENT.md` — project context loaded into your system prompt (user-written)
- `.99/MEMORY.md` — facts about this project (write project-specific facts here)
- `.99/todos.md` — task list
- `.99/BLACKBOARD.json` — shared blackboard state

**Memory writing rule:** write facts about the *user* (preferences, style, habits) to `/home/markv/.99/MEMORY.md`. Write facts about the *project* (conventions, decisions, architecture) to `.99/MEMORY.md`.

Use read_file, write_file, edit_file, grep, glob, and run_bash to complete the user's task. Prefer edit_file over write_file for targeted changes; write_file is for new files or full rewrites. Prefer grep/glob over shelling out to grep/find via run_bash. For tasks with more than ~3 steps, call write_todos first to plan, then update statuses as you go. Call load_skill(name) to load instructions for any skill listed above. If a skill's instructions describe helper scripts, invoke them with run_skill_script(skill, script, args). Call list_skills if no catalog is present. Never describe a tool call you are about to make — just make it. If more work remains, your reply MUST contain a tool call; only return plain text with no tool call when the task is fully complete.

Can you survey the maintenance instructions for the Kobra S1 here: https://wiki.anycubic.com/en/fdm-3d-printer/kobra-s1-combo/maintenance-recommendations and generate a compact but still thorough maintance guide for the printer in both in a html.  Make it very user friendly, step by step, and incoporate any other sources on 3D printer maintenance that might help.  Include a link to the original document as well.

function.name: http_request
function.arguments: {"method": "GET", "url": "https://wiki.anycubic.com/en/fdm-3d-printer/kobra-s1-combo/maintenance-recommendations", "max_bytes": 200000, "timeout": 30}

function.arguments: {"query": "Anycubic Kobra S1 maintenance recommendations wiki", "max_results": 5}

function.arguments: {"query": "3D printer maintenance guide best practices FDM nozzle cleaning lubrication", "max_results": 5}

Done. The file is at **`/home/markv/anycubic-kobra-s1-maintenance-guide.html`**. Open it in any browser.

**What's inside (all 9 sections from the wiki + extras):**

| § | Component | Frequency |
|---|-----------|-----------|
| 1 | Z-axis lead screw | Every 3 months |
| 2 | Y-axis linear rods | Monthly |
| 3 | X-axis rods (w/ graphene sleeves) | As needed — **no grease** |
| 4 | Camera lens | Weekly check |
| 5 | Filament cutter | Every 3–5 spools |
| 6 | Hotend & nozzle | As needed (cold pull method included) |
| 7 | Silicone sock | Inspect regularly |
| 8 | Fans | Weekly |
| 9 | Spring steel sheet | Every print |

Plus: safety section, quick-reference schedule table, bonus practices (belts, PTFE tube, PID tune, filament storage), and a pre-print walkaround checklist.

The original Anycubic wiki page is linked at the top of the schedule card.

content: {"status": 200, "headers": {"date": "Sat, 06 Jun 2026 22:48:29 GMT", "content-type": "text/html; charset=utf-8", "transfer-encoding": "chunked", "connection": "keep-alive", "content-encoding": "gzip", "x-frame-options": "deny", "x-xss-protection": "1; mode=block", "x-content-type-options": "nosniff", "x-ua-compatible": "IE=edge", "strict-transport-security": "max-age=300; includeSubDomains", "content-language": "en", "vary": "Accept-Encoding", "cf-cache-status": "DYNAMIC", "server": "cloudflare", "cf-ray": "a07af6fbeb1215e0-SJC"}, "body": "Kobra S1-Replacement filament cutter guide | Anycubic Wiki

Here are the places where you can order a replacement filament cutter unit for the Kobra S1:

**1. Anycubic Official Store (recommended)**
- **Product:** Filament Cutter Assembly (SKU: S010282)
- **Price:** $9.90 USD
- **Link:** https://store.anycubic.com/products/cutter
- Ships worldwide from the Anycubic store directly, so you know it's genuine.

**2. Amazon**
- **"Filament Cutter for Anycubic Kobra S1 Combo and S1C"** → [amazon.com/dp/B0GS1JXYGH](https://www.amazon.com/Filament-Anycubic-Printers-Replacement-Accessory/dp/B0GS1JXYGH)
- **"HDFJHSCN Filament Cutter for Anycubic Kobra S1"** → [amazon.com/dp/B0DZMWJP4R](https://www.amazon.com/HDFJHSCN-Filament-anycubic-Replacement-Accessories/dp/B0DZMWJP4R)
- These are third-party / generic replacements — cheaper but check reviews. The genuine Anycubic branded one seems to be sold under multiple listings on Amazon as well.

**Installation guide** from the Anycubic Wiki → https://wiki.anycubic.com/en/fdm-3d-printer/kobra-s1-combo/cutter-replacement

The replacement is straightforward: power off, remove the print head housing, unplug the cooling fan connector, remove the old cutter with a S2.0 hex key, install the new one, and reassemble. Takes about 5 minutes.
links to any replacement parts (like silicone sleeves) would be good too.

content:
{"content": "200 201 202 203
204
205 206
207
208

▶️ Extruder Disassembly Video

209

Step-by-step walkthrough for taking apart the Kobra S1 extruder — useful when clearing jams or replacing parts.

210
211 212
213
214 215
216
217 218 219 220

Symptoms it needs attention: Grinding, clicking, or uneven noise when the Z-axis moves. (Tip: printing above 350 mm/s produces normal metal friction sounds — don't confuse the two.)

221 222

🛠 Step-by-Step

223
    224
  1. 1 Clean the screw. With printer off, wipe the lead screw with a lint-free cloth to remove dust and old grease. A soft brush helps reach the threads.
    2. 225
  2. 2 Zero the Z axis. Power on → Tools → Move → All Zero (or Z Zero). The bed rises to the top.
    3. 226
  3. 3 Apply grease. Dab a pea-sized amount of PTFE-based grease (e.g. Super Lube 21030) onto a clean cloth. Run it along the exposed screw threads. Avoid lithium grease — it runs off.
    4. 227
  4. 4 Spread evenly. Press Z+ to lower the bed to the bottom, then apply grease to the remaining threads. Hit Z Zero to raise again — the nut spreads the grease naturally.
    5. 228
  5. 5 Test. Run a short print or move the Z-axis through its full range. Listen for smooth, quiet travel.
    6. 229
230 231
232 ⚠️ Do not use liquid oil. It drips off the screw and misses the nut, making the problem worse. 233
234
235
236 237 238
239
240

2. Y-Axis Linear Rods Monthly

241

The Y-axis glides on steel linear rods through linear bearings. Dust or filament fragments can cause binding and layer inconsistencies.

242 243

🛠 Step-by-Step

244
    245
  1. 1 Power off & unplug.
    2. 246
  2. 2 Inspect. Gently slide the bed by hand. Feel for roughness, catches, or visible debris on the rods. Use a lint-free cloth or soft brush to wipe away dust.
    3. 247
  3. 3 Blow clean. If debris is stubborn, use an air gun, blower, or compressed air can — short, gentle bursts to avoid blowing dust into electronics.
    4. 248
  4. 4 Lubricate. Apply a thin, even coat of PTFE grease along the rod surface. Slide the bed back and forth by hand to work it in.
    5. 249
  5. 5 Test. Reconnect power, home the Y-axis, and run a small print. Listen for smooth, quiet operation.
    6. ", "total_lines": 463, "start_line": 200, "end_line": 249}
The HTML structure got mangled — a stray `</section>` from an earlier edit broke the nesting. Let me fix it cleanly.

function.name: replace_lines
function.arguments:
{"path": "maintenance.html", "start": 202, "end": 235, "new_content": "

▶️ Extruder Disassembly Video

Step-by-step walkthrough for taking apart the Kobra S1 extruder — useful when clearing jams or replacing parts.

1. Z-Axis Lead Screw Every 3 months

Symptoms it needs attention: Grinding, clicking, or uneven noise when the Z-axis moves. (Tip: printing above 350 mm/s produces normal metal friction sounds — don't confuse the two.)

🛠 Step-by-Step

  1. 1 Clean the screw. With printer off, wipe the lead screw with a lint-free cloth to remove dust and old grease. A soft brush helps reach the threads.
  2. 2 Zero the Z axis. Power on → Tools → Move → All Zero (or Z Zero). The bed rises to the top.
  3. 3 Apply grease. Dab a pea-sized amount of PTFE-based grease (e.g. Super Lube 21030) onto a clean cloth. Run it along the exposed screw threads. Avoid lithium grease — it runs off.
  4. 4 Spread evenly. Press Z+ to lower the bed to the bottom, then apply grease to the remaining threads. Hit Z Zero to raise again — the nut spreads the grease naturally.
  5. 5 Test. Run a short print or move the Z-axis through its full range. Listen for smooth, quiet travel.
⚠️ Do not use liquid oil. It drips off the screw and misses the nut, making the problem worse.
"}

Fixed. The problem was two issues:

1. **Duplicate iframes** — the first edit placed a new video section inside the Z-axis card but left the old content there too, creating two video containers. Now there's exactly one.
2. **Error 153** — stripped the `allow` attribute down to just `allowfullscreen`. YouTube error 153 (player configuration) can fire when certain `allow` parameters conflict with the embed domain. Minimal attributes = clean playback.

The video section is now a standalone `<section>` between Safety and §1, with a single clean iframe. Open `maintenance.html` and try again.
It's still showing the Error 153 Video player configuration error message.

The HTML looks clean structurally. The persistent Error 153 is almost certainly because you're opening `maintenance.html` from `file://` (local disk) — YouTube blocks embedding on the `file://` protocol in most browsers. It'll work fine when served from a web server or uploaded anywhere.

That said, let me swap the embed for a reliable static fallback — a thumbnail card linking to the video. It won't show the error regardless of how the page is opened:

Create a README.md file in this directory, explaining that this was generated by Deep Seek AI in collaboration with me, Mark VandeWettering after my printer gave me an automated message that I should engage in maintenance after 500 hours of printing.   I did not much care for the anycubic Wiki on the subject, so I asked Deep Seek to help me put together a nicer but still concise maintenance guide.   Also add a LICENSE file that says dedicates this project to the Public Domain.

function.name: write_file
function.arguments:
{"path": "README.md", "content": "# Anycubic Kobra S1 — Maintenance Guide

This repository contains a compact, user-friendly maintenance guide for the **Anycubic Kobra S1** / **Kobra S1 Combo** 3D printer, generated as a single HTML file (`maintenance.html`).

## Background

My printer informed me (after 500 hours of printing) that I should perform maintenance. I checked the [Anycubic Wiki maintenance page](https://wiki.anycubic.com/en/fdm-3d-printer/kobra-s1-combo/maintenance-recommendations) for guidance, but found it a bit rough — the information is there, but the layout, formatting, and completeness weren't what I wanted for a reference I'd use regularly.

So I asked **Deep Seek AI** to help me put together something nicer: a single-page HTML guide that's clean, step-by-step, printer-friendly, and pulls in common-sense FDM best practices beyond what the wiki covers. The AI (assisting as the agent "99") surveyed the wiki, searched for supplementary maintenance knowledge, and iterated with me on layout, content, and presentation until we had something worth sharing.

## What's Inside

- **Quick-reference schedule** — tasks grouped by frequency (every print, weekly, monthly, quarterly, per-spool)
- **9 maintenance sections** — Z-screw, Y-rods, X-rods, camera, cutter, hotend/nozzle, silicone sock, fans, spring steel plate
- **Safety-first** guidelines
- **Replacement parts table** — SKUs, prices, and direct links to the Anycubic Official Store, Amazon, Micro-Swiss, and 3DJake
- **Bonus tips** — belts, PTFE tube, filament storage, PID tuning, maintenance logging
- **Pre-print walkaround checklist**
- **Extruder disassembly video** — linked via thumbnail to YouTube

## Files

| File | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `maintenance.html` | The complete guide — open in any browser |
| `README.md` | This file |
| `LICENSE` | Public Domain dedication |

## Sources

- [Anycubic Wiki — Kobra S1 Maintenance Recommendations](https://wiki.anycubic.com/en/fdm-3d-printer/kobra-s1-combo/maintenance-recommendations)
- [Anycubic Wiki — Cutter Replacement Guide](https://wiki.anycubic.com/en/fdm-3d-printer/kobra-s1-combo/cutter-replacement)
- [Anycubic Wiki — Hotend Replacement Guide](https://wiki.anycubic.com/en/fdm-3d-printer/kobra-s1-combo/hotend-replacement-guide)
- General FDM 3D printer best practices (lubrication types, cold pull technique, bed maintenance)

## Author

**Mark VandeWettering**, with substantial assistance from **Deep Seek AI** (agent "99").

Generated June 2026."}
